narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 79% chance you'll struggle to pass the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. Your profile shows solid strengths, like an undergraduate GPA of 3.3—which is a bit above the typical 3.21 average—and coming from a middle-income family, along with being a full-time student, female, and White, all of which align closely with the majority of applicants. However, your Year 1 school decile ranking of 3 (below the average 5.71), Year 3 decile of 2 (under the typical 5.53), and especially your LSAT score of 30.5 (well below the 36.7 average) stood out as key concerns. The model weighed your LSAT score as the single biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction, more than any other aspect of your background. Right behind it in influence were your lower Year 1 and Year 3 decile rankings, which together signaled to the model a tougher path to bar success compared to higher-performing peers. Your family income level came next in impact, followed by your undergraduate GPA, race, gender, and full-time status, each playing smaller roles but still tilting the odds toward struggle. Overall, these academic markers made your situation look riskier than most applicants, whose stronger early grades and test scores correlated with bar passage. While demographics like gender and race had minimal sway here—since your profile matches common successful students—they couldn't offset the heavier academic drags. To boost your future chances, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT with targeted prep to aim above 36, as that's the top lever for shifting predictions. Building study habits that lift your performance from those lower deciles could also help in law school and beyond, perhaps through tutoring or practice exams mimicking the bar. With dedication to these steps, you could realign your profile toward the passing patterns the model favors. Keep pushing—you've already shown GPA strength that proves your potential.
